title: Gujarati Kadhi

subs.name: equal part buttermilk; reduce water by half as buttermilk is thinner

ingredients.name: jaggery or sugar
visual_checks: jaggery gives a warmer sweetness; use sugar if preferred
ingredients.notes: jaggery gives a warmer sweetness; use sugar if preferred

subs.name: 1.5 tbsp sugar — the kadhi will be lighter in colour
neutral oil; the tempering will be less rich but still flavourful

steps.text: In a large bowl whisk together curd and besan until fully smooth with no lumps. Gradually stir in the water a little at a time to keep the mixture uniform. Add turmeric, salt, ginger, slit green chillies and jaggery; whisk again to combine.

Pour the curd mixture into a heavy-bottomed pot or saucepan set on medium heat. Stir continuously with a wooden spoon — never stop stirring at this stage — until the liquid just begins to steam and show the first signs of bubbling. Stopping early causes the curd to split into grainy curds.

Once the kadhi reaches a gentle boil, reduce the flame to low and cook uncovered, stirring every minute or two, for 10 minutes. The kadhi will gradually thicken to a pourable soup-like consistency and the besan will lose its raw taste. Taste and adjust salt and jaggery.

Heat ghee in a small saucepan on medium-high. Add mustard seeds and let them splutter. Add cumin seeds, dried red chillies, cloves, cinnamon and curry leaves; the spices will fry and crackle in about 20–25 seconds. Pour this tempering directly into the simmering kadhi — it will sizzle dramatically.
Stir the kadhi well and simmer for 2 more minutes. Finish with fresh coriander leaves and serve hot over steamed rice, with a papad and pickle on the side.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.

substitution_notes: jaggery → 1.5 tbsp sugar — the kadhi will be lighter in colour; ghee → neutral oil; the tempering will be less rich but still flavourful; curd → equal part buttermilk; reduce water by half as buttermilk is thinner
